name: Appalachian Granny Magic
description: Appalachian Granny Magic, also known as Pow-Wowing or Braucherei (though Pow-Wowing is often associated with Pennsylvania Dutch traditions and can overlap), is a folk magic tradition practiced in the Appalachian region of the United States. Its origins are a blend of European (primarily Scots-Irish and German) folk beliefs, Native American herbal knowledge, and African conjure. Granny Magic focuses on practical, everyday needs such as healing, protection, love, and prosperity. It emphasizes the use of natural remedies, charms, spells, and rituals passed down through generations, often within families. The practice is deeply rooted in the land and the cycles of nature, with a strong emphasis on intuition and common sense. Granny witches, often elder women in the community, were respected figures known for their wisdom and ability to solve problems using both practical skills and magical means. The magic is often interwoven with Christian beliefs, incorporating prayers and scripture into spells and rituals. Secrecy and oral tradition are important aspects of the practice, with knowledge shared selectively and often only with trusted family members.
subtitle: Folk Magic of the Appalachian Mountains

keyMyths.title: The Healing Herb
keyMyths.description: A granny witch discovers a new herb with powerful healing properties, passed down by Native American spirits. The herb becomes a staple in her healing practices, saving many lives.

keyMyths.title: The Protective Charm
keyMyths.description: A young woman is given a charm by her grandmother to protect her from harm during a dangerous journey. The charm works, warding off misfortune and ensuring her safe return.

sources.author: Jim Haskins
sources.description: Provides context on African conjure influences.
